q: A Hermes Skill is best described as:
choices: A markdown file that does one job, triggered by a slash command
A group of Skills loaded together
A feature only in the desktop app
explain: A Skill is a markdown file in ~/.hermes/skills/. The filename (minus .md) becomes the slash command.

q: A Skill Bundle is best described as:
choices: A named group of Skills loaded together by a single slash command
A backup of your Skills folder
explain: A Bundle is YAML that lists multiple Skills and an instruction field. One slash command loads them all in the order you specify.

q: Short answer: Per the source video, are Skill Bundles adaptive? Explain in one sentence.
explain: No — as of June 2026, Bundles run Skills in a fixed order. They do not dynamically re-order based on intermediate results.

q: Where do Hermes Skills live on your computer?
explain: ~/.hermes/skills/<name>.md. Hermes is strict about the location and the filename matching the command.

q: The instruction field in a Bundle YAML is best described as:
choices: Optional metadata that the agent ignores
A 'mini agents.md' that captures your house rules for this workflow
The model name to use for this Bundle
explain: The instruction field is where you bake in your house rules — conventions, citation style, output format. It is what makes Bundle output consistent across runs.

q: Per the source video, the best way to design a Bundle is:
choices: Pick the Skills yourself based on what you think is right
Ask Hermes to suggest the Skills, then review and commit
Use every available Skill in one Bundle
Copy a Bundle from someone else's dotfiles
explain: Ron explicitly recommends co-designing Bundles with Hermes. The agent reads its own Skill catalog and proposes a grouping; you review and refine.
